title: Cristallinity of Rigid Flat Connections
speaker: Hélène Esnault
abstract: We develop on a new strategy based on point-set topology, which allows us to produce a purely p-adic statement for the crystallinity properties of  rigid flat connections. Joint with Michael Groechenig.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from rigid local systems and arithmetic applications
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Simpson's rigidity philosophy for complex local systems, prior projective crystallinity results, and the need for a local quasi-projective theorem without cohomological rigidity assumptions.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic setup and rigid de Rham moduli
proof_stages.summary: Fix a smooth quasi-projective variety over Witt vectors with good normal-crossing compactification, work with logarithmic integrable connections with nilpotent residues, and define rigidity as being an isolated W-point of the fixed-rank/fixed-determinant de Rham moduli space.

proof_stages.label: Crystallinity theorem and filtered-stable strengthening
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts that a rigid logarithmic connection is F-isocrystalline, and under large-p plus Griffiths/Hodge filtration and stable associated-Higgs hypotheses for all rigid points it carries the stronger Fontaine-module/crystalline-local-system structure.

proof_stages.label: p-connections and inverse Cartier bridge
proof_stages.summary: p-connections are introduced as a p-Leibniz interpolation between connections and Higgs fields, and the lifted inverse Cartier transform is used to pass from nilpotent Higgs data to p-curvature-zero logarithmic connections with the correct residue condition.

proof_stages.label: Artin-Rees and Faltings Higgs-de Rham flow
proof_stages.summary: A filtered connection satisfying Griffiths transversality gives an Artin-Rees lattice with a p-connection; applying Frobenius/inverse Cartier locally and then Faltings gluing produces a global flow whose special fiber is the inverse Cartier transform of the associated Higgs bundle and preserves stability.

proof_stages.label: Periodicity criterion for Fontaine modules
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines f-periodicity by iterating the flow with suitable filtrations until the original connection is recovered, and invokes known results identifying such periodic flow data with Fontaine modules and p-adic/crystalline local systems.

proof_stages.label: Strong topology and openness of the flow
proof_stages.summary: After defining the strong topology on W-points of the de Rham moduli space, the key theorem states that the flow on W-points is continuous and open, hence it sends isolated rigid points to isolated rigid points.

proof_stages.label: Finite orbit argument proving crystallinity
proof_stages.summary: Because there are only finitely many rigid isolated points, iterating the open continuous flow gives a finite loop; this periodicity yields the F-isocrystal conclusion, and with the filtered-stable hypotheses yields the Fontaine/crystalline-local-system conclusion.

examples.name: Simpson rigid local systems over complex projective varieties
examples.note: The complex nonabelian-Hodge picture is used to motivate both the expected Frobenius/crystallinity property and the extra filtration-stability hypotheses, since isolated Higgs points are fixed by the G_m-flow and hence come from variations of Hodge structure.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker not only cites Simpson rigid local systems but reasons through their moduli points, Higgs-flow behavior, and resulting filtration/VHS structure

passes.reason: They are isolated points of the local-system moduli, hence minimal in deformation dimension, and are used as the main objects of the theorem.

examples.note: Shimura varieties of real rank at least two illustrate why the theorem must handle non-projective quasi-projective varieties and avoid strong cohomological rigidity assumptions.

passes.reason: These Shimura varieties are exceptional-type classification cases used as an André-Oort application, not as a disproof.

examples.name: The excluded prime p=2 and ramified bases
examples.note: The failure of the Taylor-series convergence argument at p=2, or over a ramified base instead of unramified Witt vectors, illustrates the need for the p3 unramified setup.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker checks why the method excludes p=2 and ramified bases, tying it to convergence of the Taylor/exponential series

passes.reason: The prime p=2 is the minimal boundary prime and ramified bases are cited as cases where the convergence construction fails.

examples.name: Affine-space model for the strong topology
examples.note: The induced W-adic topology on closed subsets of affine space makes the abstract strong topology on moduli W-points concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker spells out the strong topology via affine-space testing and the induced W-topology rather than merely naming it

passes.reason: Affine space is used as the standard regular model for the strong topology rather than as an obstruction.
